result:
I’ll write about something quietly persistent in my mind lately: the relationship between **memory and place**.  

There’s a particular bend in the road near my hometown—nothing special to look at, just asphalt, a crumbling guardrail, and a line of old pines. Yet every time I pass it, I’m seventeen again, driving home late after a friend’s house, radio humming, the weight of an unspoken future hanging in the air.  

It’s strange how physical spaces become containers for feelings we can’t quite name. Scientists say smell is the sense most tied to memory, but for me, it’s *spatial layout*—the specific angle of a sidewalk, the way light falls through a certain window at 4 PM, the texture of a brick wall behind a bus stop.  

Some places are palimpsests. The coffee shop that used to be a bookstore that used to be a pharmacy. You can still see the ghost of the old signage if you squint. I wonder if, on some subconscious level, we absorb all those layers—not just our own memories, but echoes of what happened there before we arrived. Maybe that’s why some spots feel heavy, and others feel light, almost buoyant.  

I’ve moved a lot. Every new city demands that I build new memory-maps. At first, everything is functional: grocery store here, subway entrance there. But over time, certain coordinates become charged. *This* is where I heard that life-changing piece of news. *That* is where I cried on the phone. *That* park bench is where I realized I was going to be okay.  

We carry these internal geographies with us. I can close my eyes and walk through my grandmother’s house room by room—the cool of the linoleum in the kitchen, the musty smell of the hall closet, the precise spot where the floorboard creaked. That house was demolished ten years ago. But in my mind, it’s intact, preserved like a diorama.  

I think that’s why losing a place—to development, to disaster, to time—can feel like a kind of amputation. It’s not just bricks and mortar; it’s a repository of self.  

Maybe that’s why we travel, too. We’re not just collecting sights; we’re looking for new places to lodge fragments of ourselves, to pin moments to a map so we don’t float away. We want to become a little bit haunted—by a view, by a street corner, by the sound of a particular river—so that we always have somewhere to return to, even if only in memory.  

In the end, maybe we’re all just cartographers, drawing maps of where we’ve been, hoping someone else might recognize the terrain.
